flashcards.front: What is storage management in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Storage management is the OS function that controls how data is stored, accessed, and maintained on computer systems, including both main memory (RAM) and secondary storage devices like hard disks and SSDs.

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of storage managed by OS?
flashcards.back: PRIMARY STORAGE (main memory/RAM) - volatile, fast, directly accessed by CPU; and SECONDARY STORAGE (hard disks, SSDs, optical media) - non-volatile, slower, persistent.

flashcards.front: What is disk scheduling and why is it needed?
flashcards.back: Disk scheduling is the process of determining the order in which disk I/O requests are served. It is needed to minimize seek time, reduce rotational latency, and improve overall system throughput.
FCFS (First-Come-First-Served) processes requests in the order they arrive. It is simple and fair but does not optimize for seek time, potentially causing long waiting times.
SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) selects the request closest to the current head position. It provides better performance than FCFS but may cause starvation for distant requests.
SCAN algorithm moves the disk head in one direction servicing all requests until reaching the end, then reverses direction. Also called elevator algorithm as it mimics an elevator.
C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) moves the head in one direction servicing requests, then jumps back to the beginning without servicing requests on the return trip, providing uniform wait time.
A file system is an OS component that provides an abstraction for storing, retrieving, and organizing files on secondary storage, managing metadata like file names, permissions, and timestamps.

flashcards.front: What are the three file allocation methods?
flashcards.back: CONTIGUOUS ALLOCATION - files stored in consecutive blocks; LINKED ALLOCATION - each block points to next; INDEXED ALLOCATION - uses index blocks to store pointers to data blocks.

flashcards.front: What is free space management in OS?
flashcards.back: Free space management tracks which storage blocks are available for allocation. Common techniques include bit vector (bitmap), linked list, grouping, and counting methods.
A buffer cache is a portion of main memory that stores recently accessed disk blocks, reducing disk I/O by serving frequently requested data from memory instead of disk.
Swap space is a portion of secondary storage used as an extension of RAM when physical memory is full. The OS moves inactive memory pages to swap space to free RAM for active processes.

flashcards.front: What is RAID and its levels?
flashcards.back: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) combines multiple physical disks for reliability and performance. Common levels: RAID 0 (striping), RAID 1 (mirroring), RAID 5 (parity), RAID 6 (double parity).

Disk partitioning divides a physical disk into logical sections called partitions. Each partition can hold a separate file system or be used for different purposes like dual-booting operating systems.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between logical and physical file organization?
flashcards.back: Physical file organization refers to how data is actually stored on disk (contiguous, linked, indexed), while logical organization refers to how users perceive data (hierarchical, network, sequential).
